html:
TSE:5256 Fusic Co., Ltd.

At the May 15, 2026 close of ¥2,182, Fusic is a ¥2.8 billion market cap, sub-$20 million Japanese microcap with about ¥480 million of cash, no financial debt, and an enterprise value around ¥2.3 billion. The screen says “expensive”: FY2026 company EPS guidance is only ¥13.50, so the headline forward P/E is over 160x. That is exactly the opportunity. FY2026 is not a normalized earnings year. It is an intentionally depressed investment year.

The market is treating Fusic like a tiny, promotional systems integrator that had one good year and then destroyed margins. The better read is that FY2026 is a deliberate reset from a profitable, founder-led cloud/AI services company into a higher-capacity AI-native integrator, MSP, space-software contractor, and selective roll-up platform. That sounds messy, which is why it is cheap. But the operating data from the May 12 Q3 materials show the transition is not just a story: Q3 cumulative revenue grew 22.1% to ¥1.78 billion, Q3 standalone revenue hit a record ¥687 million, and all three revenue lines are growing.

The key mispricing is that the income statement now looks worse because Fusic is expensing the buildout before the revenue fully arrives. SG&A rose 57.8% in the first nine months, crushing operating profit from ¥223 million to ¥130 million, while gross margin only slipped from 40.7% to 40.1%. That is a very different signal from a structurally impaired business. The company did not lose pricing power. It hired, opened Tokyo, added AI tooling, invested in space-sector credibility, pushed product sales, and began M&A sourcing.

The service mix matters. In the first nine months of FY2026, Cross Technology revenue was ¥962 million, up 19.3%; MSP was ¥680 million, up 26.8%; products were ¥138 million, up 19.9%. Q3 was stronger: MSP grew 41.8% year over year, driven by AWS resale to public-sector customers, while Cross Technology grew 27.0%. The prior bullish thesis overstated this as a clean SaaS-like recurring shift. It is not that simple. AWS resale can carry lower gross margins than bespoke development. But it creates sticky billing relationships, account control, and a natural path into managed services, security, Claude/Bedrock implementation, and AI workflow projects. The resale line is the land-and-expand wedge, not the whole margin story.

The Anthropic angle is also more real than a buzzword. Fusic was accepted into Anthropic’s Authorized Reseller Program for Amazon Bedrock, allowing customers to use Claude directly through Fusic-provided AWS accounts. For a company already positioned around universities, public-sector entities, research institutions, and cloud infrastructure, this lowers procurement friction. The near-term revenue impact is stated as immaterial, but the strategic value is higher: it makes Fusic a one-stop AWS plus Claude implementation partner for customers that cannot easily stitch together AI vendors, cloud billing, security, and operations by themselves.

The space business is the ignored option. Fusic is not building rockets or satellites. It is building the cloud software layer around satellite operations and data. That is a better niche for a 120-person company. It has JAXA-related work, QPS Research dashboard work, Kick Space Technologies satellite operations infrastructure, and six companies/eight projects in progress as of Q3. This is still small, but it is the kind of niche where credentials compound. Hardware-heavy space startups need software, cloud architecture, monitoring, data storage, and operational dashboards, and Fusic can sell that capability without taking launch risk.

The weirdest part, the investment in Amanosato, may actually explain management’s larger ambition. The market can easily dismiss a cloud company buying into craft brewing as unfocused. The company’s stated logic is that AI-native development plus owned domain data and operating know-how creates more value than pure client work. That may be too abstract, and it deserves skepticism. But the deeper point is that management is trying to escape the “labor hours for client projects” ceiling. The risk is diworsification; the upside is Fusic learning to apply its tech stack to owned or partially owned vertical businesses.

Founder alignment is unusually strong. As of the latest major shareholder data, founders Noutomi and Hamasaki each own 21.63%, and their asset management companies own another 20.52% combined. They did sell a small portion through an off-floor trade in 2025 to improve liquidity, but they remain controlling economic actors. This is not an orphaned management team optimizing for salary.

The stock is obscure, illiquid, and retail frustration is obvious. Yahoo Finance board chatter focuses on low liquidity, “small IR,” failed short-term pops, and disappointment that the stock does not move despite AI/space news. Kabu Berry’s March 2026 retail IR transcript shows management doing the right small-cap IR work, but engagement remains niche. That matters because the stock is not crowded. The current shareholder base seems tired, not euphoric.

The forward valuation is the point. Management’s FY2028 vision is at least ¥5.0 billion of revenue and ¥750 million of operating profit. Current EV is about 0.46x that revenue target and 3.1x that operating profit target. Discount the plan heavily and the stock is still inexpensive. If FY2028 revenue reaches only ¥4.0 billion and operating margin normalizes to 10 to 12%, Fusic can produce roughly ¥280 to ¥330 million of net income, or ¥220 to ¥260 per share. A 17 to 20x multiple on that gets to roughly ¥4,000 to ¥5,200 per share without heroic assumptions.

The downside is that the FY2028 plan depends on execution, hiring, AI productivity gains, and some M&A contribution. If the Tokyo office only adds revenue at low resale margins, AI tooling fails to improve delivery capacity, and space remains reference work rather than repeatable revenue, FY2026 may not be a temporary margin trough. In that case, the stock deserves to trade closer to book plus a modest services-business value.

But the asymmetry is attractive because the market is valuing Fusic as if the margin collapse is structural while the actual evidence points to investment-led suppression. Revenue is accelerating, gross margin is holding, the balance sheet is clean, founders remain heavily exposed, and the company has multiple underwritten growth vectors that are too small and strange for institutional screens.

Bear case: ¥1,400, down 36%. FY2026 proves to be the start of lower structural margins, M&A distracts management, and the market values the company at roughly 1.4x book plus limited credit for growth.

Base case: ¥4,300, up 97%. Revenue compounds into the ¥3.8 to ¥4.2 billion range, operating margins rebuild to 10 to 12%, and normalized EPS reaches roughly ¥230. A 18 to 19x multiple is enough.

Bull case: ¥7,800, up 257%. Management gets close to the FY2028 vision of ¥5.0 billion plus revenue and ¥750 million plus operating profit. Net income approaches ¥500 million, EPS approaches ¥390, and the market pays 20x for a founder-led AI/cloud/space software compounder.
